planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens against a masonry wall, fence, or shaded slope in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial to full shade in spring; creeping fig adheres by adhesive rootlets and requires no support structure — it anchors directly to masonry, stucco, and wood.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container specimens against masonry, stucco, or wood surfaces in partial to full shade; creeping fig is a vigorous East Asian climbing fig that attaches to vertical surfaces by tiny adhesive rootlets, blanketing walls and fences in a dense, neat carpet of small, heart-shaped, dark-green, juvenile leaves — one of the few true shade vines that thrives in deep shade and provides the distinctive ivy-covered wall effect so prized in formal garden design. Juvenile foliage is small and refined; if the vine is not trimmed, the older climbing stems eventually produce larger, leathery, elliptic adult leaves and even figs, but most gardeners trim annually to maintain the juvenile foliage texture. Excellent for covering bare masonry walls, garden structures, and shaded embankments in Gulf Coast landscapes. Can become very large and difficult to remove once established — roots deeply into masonry. Tolerates brief cold snaps to 15–20°F.

planting.note: Divide rosette offsets or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade after the last frost; oyster plant tolerates poor soil, drought, and salt spray, making it one of the most useful and indestructible shade groundcovers for Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Divide rosette offsets or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial to full shade; oyster plant (Moses in the cradle, boat lily) is a Central American native that forms dense, suckering rosettes of stiff, lance-shaped leaves that are glossy dark green on the upper surface and rich purple-violet on the lower surface — a two-tone combination that makes it one of the most distinctive tropical groundcovers available. Small white flowers are cradled within paired, boat-shaped bracts in the leaf axils (giving the common name Moses in the cradle). The plant is extremely tough — tolerant of drought, poor soil, salt spray, and deep shade — and spreads by offsets to form dense, weed-suppressing groundcover colonies in difficult spots under trees and on shaded embankments. An essential low-maintenance groundcover for Gulf Coast zone 8–11 landscapes. Tender to hard frost but rebounds from root in zone 8; root-hardy to approximately 25°F.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in water or moist perlite and plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; bloodleaf develops its most intense coloration in bright indirect light to partial shade — deep shade reduces color intensity while full hot sun can scorch the leaves.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings in water or moist perlite and plant in moist, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; bloodleaf (beefsteak plant) is a South American native grown for its spectacular, glowing crimson to deep wine-red foliage — the leaves are broadly notched and suffused with vivid red-pink pigmentation that makes the plant appear to glow in the summer border and container garden. It is used as a vivid foliage accent and is excellent for creating high-contrast combinations with yellow, orange, and white-flowered companions. Pinch growing tips regularly to maintain a compact, bushy form and prevent leggy growth. Root cuttings in water in autumn before frost to overwinter indoors as a houseplant; plants can grow for several years in warm indoor conditions. In zones 10–12 it persists as a tender perennial sub-shrub. An irreplaceable element of Victorian-era bedding designs, now enjoying renewed popularity in mixed container arrangements.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Divide basal suckers in spring or plant large container-grown specimens in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; white bird of paradise grows slowly to tree proportions and requires ample space — allow at least 10 feet on all sides for a mature specimen.

directions: Divide basal suckers or plant large container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; white bird of paradise is the giant tree-form relative of the familiar orange bird of paradise (S. reginae), developing massive, paddle-shaped, blue-green leaves up to 6 feet long on tall, bamboo-like trunks — producing a dramatic, tropical architectural effect in the landscape. The flowers are spectacular when they appear: large, boat-shaped spathe bracts of deep purple-blue cradle blue-and-white flower petals. The plant is extensively used as a dramatic indoor specimen (the most popular tropical foliage plant in US interior landscaping) and as an outdoor landscape focal point in zones 10–12. Tolerates partial shade and indoor low light better than most tropical trees, though outdoor plants in full sun are most vigorous. Outstanding as a large-scale specimen in courtyards, entrances, and pool gardens in zone 10–12 landscapes.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring when new growth emerges or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; queen lily thrives in the dappled shade under trees and is one of the most shade-tolerant and garden-worthy of all the ornamental curcumas.

directions: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; queen lily (hidden lily) is a Malaysian ginger relative that is one of the most effective shade ornamentals for the warm garden — the broad, tropical-looking, bright-green leaves emerge in spring and rise to 2–3 feet, and in summer the inflorescences emerge directly from the ground on separate stems, displaying stacked, overlapping, salmon-pink to rosy-pink bracts surrounding white to creamy flowers that appear 'hidden' within the bract structure, giving the common name hidden lily. The display is remarkably effective emerging from the ground surrounded by the leafy canopy. Goes fully dormant in winter (rhizomes are cold-hardy to zone 8). An excellent companion for hostas, gingers, and ferns in the summer shade garden. Rhizomes can be lifted and stored indoors in colder zones.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
